words.shortDefinition: Process of evaluating a system to confirm it meets requirements.
words.simpleDefinition: The process of running a program or system to check whether it behaves correctly and catches problems before they reach users.
words.longDefinition: Software testing is the process of evaluating and verifying that a software product or application does what it is supposed to do, while identifying bugs and ensuring quality.
words.techContext: Software testing is a critical part of the SDLC, helping to prevent costly production outages and ensuring a good user experience.
words.exampleSentence: We dedicated two weeks of the project timeline to software testing to ensure the new payment feature was robust.
words.connector: Think of software testing like taste-testing a dish before serving it — you check for problems while you can still fix them, not after the guest complains.

words.shortDefinition: Testing individual components or functions in isolation.
words.simpleDefinition: A test that checks one small, isolated piece of code — a single function or method — in complete isolation from everything else.
words.longDefinition: A unit test is a level of software testing where individual units or components of a software are tested, typically by developers during the coding phase.
words.techContext: Writing a unit test for every small function ensures that the basic building blocks of our application are working correctly.
words.exampleSentence: I wrote a unit test to verify that the math utility correctly handles negative numbers.
words.connector: Think of a unit test like testing a single light bulb before installing it — you plug it into a socket alone, not into the whole house, to verify it works by itself.

words.shortDefinition: A statement that a certain condition is true in a test.
words.simpleDefinition: A statement in a test that declares what the correct result should be — if the code produces a different result, the assertion fails and the test fails.
words.longDefinition: An assertion is a boolean expression at a specific point in a program which will be true unless there is a bug; in testing, it's used to compare the actual result with the expected result.
words.techContext: Every test case must have at least one assertion to determine whether the test passed or failed.
words.exampleSentence: The test failed because the assertion expected the status code to be 200, but it received 500.
words.etymology: From Latin 'asserere', to claim or maintain.
words.connector: Think of an assertion like a referee's call — you state what the right answer is, and if reality disagrees, you call it out immediately.

words.shortDefinition: A collection of related test cases.
words.simpleDefinition: A collection of related tests grouped together so they can be run as a batch and their results reported together.
words.longDefinition: A test suite is a container that has a set of tests which help testers in executing and reporting the test execution status.
words.techContext: We grouped all our login-related tests into a single test suite to make it easier to run them together.
words.exampleSentence: The full test suite takes about ten minutes to run in our CI/CD pipeline.
words.connector: Think of a test suite like a car's MOT checklist — a bundle of individual checks (brakes, lights, emissions) run together to give one overall pass/fail verdict.

words.shortDefinition: A specific scenario used to test a feature.
words.simpleDefinition: A single, documented scenario that checks one specific behaviour: given these inputs, the system should produce this output.
words.longDefinition: A test case is a set of conditions or variables under which a tester will determine whether a system under test satisfies requirements or works correctly.
words.techContext: I added a new test case to cover the edge case where a user enters an extremely long password.
words.exampleSentence: The QA team documented over fifty test cases for the new checkout process.
words.connector: Think of a test case like a recipe trial — you define exact ingredients (inputs), follow the steps, and check whether the dish matches what the recipe promised.

words.shortDefinition: A tool that executes tests and reports the results.
words.simpleDefinition: The tool that discovers and executes tests, collects results, and reports which passed and which failed.
words.longDefinition: A test runner is a software tool that automatically picks up and executes test files, provides a dashboard for the results, and can often watch files for changes.
words.techContext: Jest is a popular test runner for JavaScript that provides a fast and interactive testing experience.
words.exampleSentence: The test runner highlighted the failing tests in red, making it easy to see where the regression occurred.
words.connector: Think of a test runner like an invigilator at an exam — they hand out papers (load tests), time the session (execute), and collect and mark scripts (report results).

codeExample.code:
$ npm test
# This command invokes the test runner

words.shortDefinition: Testing software by interacting with it as a human user.
words.simpleDefinition: Testing done by a human who interacts with the software directly, following a plan or exploring freely, rather than running automated scripts.
words.longDefinition: Manual testing is the process of manually executing test cases without the use of automation tools to find bugs and ensure the software meets user expectations.
words.techContext: While we automate as much as possible, manual testing is still essential for exploratory testing and verifying UI aesthetics.
words.exampleSentence: Manual testing revealed that the 'Submit' button was slightly misaligned on mobile screens.
words.connector: Think of manual testing like a food critic visiting a restaurant — a real person experiences it, notices things no recipe check would catch, and writes a verdict based on the full experience.

codeExample.code: // No code; involves a human clicking through the app

words.shortDefinition: Using software tools to execute tests automatically.
words.simpleDefinition: Tests written as code that a machine runs on demand, giving fast, repeatable feedback without human involvement for each execution.
words.longDefinition: Automated testing is a process where software tools are used to execute pre-scripted tests on a software application before it is released into production.
words.techContext: Automated testing allows us to run thousands of checks every time we push new code, giving us confidence that we haven't broken existing features.
words.exampleSentence: The shift toward automated testing allowed our team to ship updates daily instead of monthly.
words.connector: Think of automated testing like a smoke detector — once set up, it runs by itself constantly and alerts you the moment something is wrong, no human check needed.

words.shortDefinition: Testing where the internal structure of the item is unknown to the tester.
words.simpleDefinition: Testing where the tester only knows the inputs and expected outputs — not the internal code — and checks the system entirely from the outside.
words.longDefinition: Black-box testing focuses on the inputs and outputs of the software without any knowledge of the internal code or logic.
words.techContext: Black-box testing is often performed by a dedicated QA team who only has access to the user interface and requirements.
words.exampleSentence: In black-box testing, we only care that the login works, not how the authentication function is implemented.
words.etymology: Metaphor for a 'black box' where you cannot see what is inside.
words.connector: Think of black-box testing like testing a vending machine — you put money in, press a button, and verify what comes out, without ever opening the machine to see its wiring.

words.shortDefinition: Testing where the internal structure of the item is known to the tester.
words.simpleDefinition: Testing where the tester has full knowledge of the internal code and designs tests that exercise specific paths, branches, and conditions inside it.
words.longDefinition: White-box testing (also known as clear-box testing) is a method where the tester has access to the internal code and can design tests based on the logic and paths within the software.
words.techContext: Developers use white-box testing to ensure that all branches of an 'if/else' statement are correctly exercised by the test suite.
words.exampleSentence: White-box testing is useful for finding hidden logic errors that might not be apparent from the UI alone.
words.etymology: Metaphor for a 'white' or 'clear' box where you can see the internal contents.
words.connector: Think of white-box testing like a plumber inspecting pipes with the wall open — they see exactly where each pipe runs and test each joint directly.

words.shortDefinition: Retesting a system after changes to ensure old features still work.
words.simpleDefinition: Re-running existing tests after a change to verify that nothing that previously worked has been accidentally broken.
words.longDefinition: Regression testing is the practice of running previous tests after a software update to confirm that new changes have not negatively affected existing functionality.
words.techContext: We run a full regression testing cycle before every major release to catch any accidental bugs introduced during the sprint.
words.exampleSentence: The regression testing caught a bug where the new profile update feature accidentally disabled the password reset link.
words.connector: Think of regression testing like a health check after surgery — you run standard tests to confirm that fixing one problem hasn't created a new one elsewhere.

words.shortDefinition: Preliminary testing to reveal simple failures severe enough to reject a prospective release.
words.simpleDefinition: A quick, shallow pass over the most critical features to verify that the build is stable enough for deeper testing — the 'does it turn on?' check.
words.longDefinition: Smoke testing is a quick, high-level test run to ensure the most critical functions of an application work (e.g., does it even start?) before proceeding with more detailed testing.
words.techContext: If the smoke testing fails, we immediately reject the build and send it back to the developers to save time.
words.exampleSentence: The smoke testing pass confirmed that the core dashboard and login are working after the server migration.
words.etymology: Metaphor from plumbing/electronics, where smoke is used to find obvious leaks or shorts.
words.connector: Think of smoke testing like turning on a new appliance for the first time — if smoke comes out, you stop; if it powers up cleanly, you move to the full test plan.

words.shortDefinition: Quick testing to verify that a specific bug fix works as expected.
words.simpleDefinition: A narrow, focused check performed after a small change to confirm that the specific area touched still works correctly before running the full test suite.
words.longDefinition: Sanity testing is a subset of regression testing used to verify that a specific feature or bug fix works as intended after a change, without running the entire suite.
words.techContext: After the developer fixed the logout issue, I performed a sanity testing check to make sure the fix worked before starting the full regression suite.
words.exampleSentence: Sanity testing showed that the spelling error on the landing page was corrected.
words.etymology: From Latin 'sanitas', health or soundness of mind.
words.connector: Think of sanity testing like checking your wallet is still in your pocket after jostling through a crowd — a quick targeted check, not a full inventory.

words.shortDefinition: An error, flaw, or fault in a computer program that causes it to produce an incorrect result.
words.simpleDefinition: An unintended behaviour in software — where the program does something different from what it was designed or expected to do.
words.longDefinition: A bug is an informal term for a defect or error in software that prevents it from working as expected or causes it to behave in unintended ways.
words.techContext: We use a tracking system to manage our bugs, prioritizing them based on their impact on the end-user.
words.exampleSentence: The developer fixed the bug that was causing the app to crash whenever the user uploaded a PNG file.
words.etymology: Originally referring to physical insects causing malfunctions in early mechanical computers.
words.connector: Think of a bug like a typo in a recipe — the instructions say '1 cup of salt' when they meant '1 teaspoon', and anyone who follows them literally gets an inedible dish.

words.shortDefinition: A deviation from the intended requirement or specification.
words.simpleDefinition: A flaw in the software that causes it to deviate from its requirements — more formal than 'bug', often used in QA reporting.
words.longDefinition: A defect is a professional term for any flaw in a software product that causes it to fail to meet its requirements or specifications.
words.techContext: The QA analyst reported a high-priority defect because the system was not calculating the sales tax correctly.
words.exampleSentence: The root cause of the defect was found to be a misunderstanding of the business requirements.
words.etymology: From Latin 'defectus', a failure or lack.
words.connector: Think of a defect like a crack in a product found during factory quality control — it's documented, assigned a severity, and tracked until fixed before shipment.

words.shortDefinition: A human action that produces an incorrect result.
words.simpleDefinition: A mistake made by a person — a developer misunderstanding a requirement or making a coding mistake — that then causes a defect in the software.
words.longDefinition: In testing terminology, an error is a mistake made by a person (e.g., a developer or designer) that leads to a fault or bug in the software.
words.techContext: A simple syntax error in the configuration file caused the entire production server to go offline.
words.exampleSentence: The error was introduced during the last-minute refactor of the authentication module.
words.etymology: From Latin 'error', a wandering or mistake.
words.connector: Think of an error like a builder misreading a blueprint — the mistake was human; the crack in the wall that results is the defect.

words.shortDefinition: The inability of a system to perform its required function.
words.simpleDefinition: The observable moment when software behaves incorrectly at runtime — what the user or test sees when a defect is triggered.
words.longDefinition: Failure is the actual manifestation of a bug or defect during the execution of the software, resulting in the system not doing what it is supposed to do.
words.techContext: The system failure occurred when the database ran out of memory, causing all active sessions to drop.
words.exampleSentence: A single bug in the calculation engine led to a critical failure in the end-of-month reporting.
words.etymology: From Old French 'faillir', to be lacking.
words.connector: Think of failure like a car not starting — the failure is what you experience; the defect is the dead battery underneath causing it.

codeExample.code:
// System Output
> Process exited with code 1 (Failure)

words.shortDefinition: The hardware and software setup used to execute tests.
words.simpleDefinition: A controlled setup — servers, databases, configuration — where tests are run, kept separate from production so testing can't harm real users or data.
words.longDefinition: A test environment (or test bed) is a controlled setup that mimics the production environment as closely as possible, allowing for accurate testing without affecting real users.
words.techContext: We need to refresh the data in our test environment to match the latest schema from the production database.
words.exampleSentence: The bug was only reproducible in the staging test environment, not on the developer's local machine.
words.etymology: Combination of 'test' and 'environment' (Old French 'environner', to surround).
words.connector: Think of a test environment like a film set — it looks like a real location but is separate, so you can knock things over, reshoot, and try again without consequence.

words.shortDefinition: Information used as input to execute test cases.
words.simpleDefinition: The input values, database records, or files used when running tests — carefully chosen to cover normal cases, edge cases, and invalid inputs.
words.longDefinition: Test data is specifically designed or selected input used to verify that a feature handles various scenarios, including valid, invalid, and edge-case inputs.
words.techContext: We use a script to generate synthetic test data, ensuring we don't use real customer information in our development environments.
words.exampleSentence: I need more test data to verify that the system can handle users with non-English characters in their names.
words.connector: Think of test data like props for a rehearsal — you prepare specific objects and scenarios so the actors (code) have everything they need to run through each scene.

words.shortDefinition: The outcome predicted by the requirements if the system works correctly.
words.simpleDefinition: What the system should produce, given a specific input and starting state — defined before the test is run and used to judge whether it passed.
words.longDefinition: An expected result is a predetermined outcome for a test case based on the software documentation, used as a benchmark for determining if a test passed or failed.
words.techContext: The test failed because the actual result was 'null', while the expected result was a valid user object.
words.exampleSentence: Always document the expected result clearly in the test plan to avoid confusion among the QA team.
words.connector: Think of expected result like the answer key for an exam — written before the papers are marked, it determines whether each answer is right or wrong.

words.shortDefinition: The real outcome produced by the software when a test is run.
words.simpleDefinition: What the system actually produced when the test was run — compared against the expected result to determine pass or fail.
words.longDefinition: The actual result is the observable behavior or output of an application during a test run, which is compared against the expected result to determine success.
words.techContext: When a bug is found, the tester must document exactly what the actual result was to help the developer diagnose the issue.
words.exampleSentence: The discrepancy between the actual result and the expected result confirmed the existence of a bug in the calculation logic.
words.connector: Think of actual result like a student's written answer — compared to the answer key (expected result) to see if they match.

words.shortDefinition: The status of a test based on whether the actual result matches the expected result.
words.simpleDefinition: The binary verdict of a test: pass if the actual result matches the expected result, fail if it doesn't.
words.longDefinition: Pass/Fail is the binary outcome of a test case; it is marked as 'Pass' if the system behaves as expected and 'Fail' if it does not.
words.techContext: Our CI pipeline automatically blocks the deployment if any test in the suite is marked as a fail.
words.exampleSentence: The final report showed a 95% pass rate for the regression suite.
words.connector: Think of pass/fail like a product inspection stamp — the item either meets the quality bar (pass) or it doesn't (fail); no partial credit.

words.shortDefinition: The practice of preventing defects in software products.
words.simpleDefinition: Quality Assurance — the ongoing process of monitoring and improving the software development process to prevent defects from being introduced in the first place.
words.longDefinition: Quality Assurance (QA) is a systematic way of monitoring and evaluating the various aspects of a project, service, or facility to ensure that standards of quality are being met.
words.techContext: QA is focused on the process of software development, ensuring we are using the right tools and methodologies to prevent bugs from occurring.
words.exampleSentence: The QA team introduced new coding standards to improve the overall reliability of our codebase.
words.connector: Think of QA like a chef setting up kitchen standards — rather than tasting every dish at the end, they write recipes, train staff, and check hygiene throughout so fewer bad dishes ever get cooked.

words.shortDefinition: A specific capability or condition that the software must meet.
words.simpleDefinition: A formally stated need or condition that the software must satisfy — the agreed specification that tests are written against.
words.longDefinition: A requirement is a documented need or desire that a software product must fulfill to be considered successful by its users or stakeholders.
words.techContext: The system failed the test because it didn't meet the requirement that all passwords must contain at least one special character.
words.exampleSentence: We mapped every test case back to a specific requirement to ensure full test coverage.
words.etymology: From Latin 'requirere', to seek after or ask.
words.connector: Think of a requirement like a client's brief to an architect — it defines what the building must do before a single brick is laid.

words.shortDefinition: The activity of checking software for defects before release.
words.simpleDefinition: Quality Control — the inspection activity that checks finished products or deliverables against defined standards to find defects.
words.longDefinition: Quality Control (QC) is the set of activities focused on identifying defects in the actual products produced, typically through testing and inspection.
words.techContext: While QA focuses on the process, QC focuses on the output—making sure the actual app we built matches the requirements.
words.exampleSentence: The final QC check found a few minor bugs that the automated suite missed.
words.connector: Think of QC like a final check at a factory production line — each finished item is inspected before it leaves, catching anything that doesn't meet the spec.
